narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 44.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which was above their threshold for approval and led to the rejection of your application. Your profile shows strong undergraduate performance with a 3.7 GPA—well above the typical 3.21 average—but lower rankings in your first-year decile at 1st place out of 10 and third-year at 2nd, compared to school averages around the middle of the pack. Your LSAT score of 28 is also notably below the dataset average of about 37, while your middle family income aligns closely with typical applicants, and as a full-time female Asian student, you fit some common demographic patterns, though Asian applicants make up just 6% of the group. When the model evaluated your application, the biggest factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your third-year decile ranking, as being in the lower end signaled higher risk compared to peers. Right behind that was your first-year decile, similarly highlighting early academic struggles relative to the middle-of-the-class norm. Your LSAT score ranked third in influence, with its below-average result further tipping the scales toward concern about bar exam readiness. Family income came next, where your middle bracket subtly contributed to the risk assessment in line with dataset patterns. Your racial background as Asian followed closely, influencing the prediction based on group trends. Even your undergraduate GPA, despite being a strength above average, played a supporting role in pushing toward failure, possibly due to how it contrasted with your law school grades. Your full-time status had a very minor push toward risk, while your gender as female provided the only slight counterbalance favoring success. Overall, your lower law school deciles and LSAT stood out as the top concerns when compared to successful bar passers, who typically rank higher academically. To improve your future chances, focus on boosting your law school performance to climb those decile rankings through better study habits or tutoring, and consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the 37 average. With targeted effort on these changeable areas, you could shift the model's view toward predicting bar exam success next time. Keep pushing—you've already shown undergraduate strength that bodes well with refinement.
